frameworks.name: bounded diffusion (sequential sampling)
frameworks.one_line: evidence accumulated from stored sensory memory until a decision bound is reached

frameworks.name: working memory
frameworks.one_line: memory retains sensory evidence based on its future utility for upcoming choices

frameworks.name: perceptual decision-making
frameworks.one_line: categorical decisions about noisy sensory evidence guide subsequent action

core_question: When perceptual decisions are uncoupled from actions, do humans postpone categorization until response options appear, and what computation supports the delay?
key_assumptions: stored sensory evidence can be re-sampled after stimulus offset
a bounded diffusion process governs accumulation to a decision
categorical commitment can be deferred until choice targets are known
manipulations: delay between random-dot motion stimulus and choice-target onset
uncoupling of perceptual category from motor response mapping
key_findings: subjects with higher decision accuracy showed prolonged deliberation after target onset
decision-report time depended on motion-evidence strength
accuracy and report-time patterns were captured by a bounded diffusion model sampling from memory
individual differences in motion-evidence integration ability were observed
interpretations: decisions can be postponed by sequentially sampling stored sensory information once response options appear
working memory strategically retains evidence based on its future decision utility
